title: Cat Pupils Dilated and Not Responding to Light: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Fixed dilated pupils in cats can indicate a medical emergency. Learn why this happens and when to seek immediate veterinary care for your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Fixed and dilated pupils are a high-urgency medical emergency in cats and require immediate veterinary evaluation to prevent permanent blindness or neurological damage.

What does it mean if my cat has fixed and dilated pupils?

When a cat's pupils remain large (dilated) and fail to shrink (constrict) when exposed to bright light, it is a condition known as fixed mydriasis. This suggests that the normal neurological or physical reflex of the eye is being interrupted. This can affect one eye or both and is often a sign of a serious underlying health issue affecting the eyes, the brain, or the blood pressure.

Should I take my cat to the emergency vet if their pupils are dilated?

  • High Urgency: If your cat’s pupils are fixed and non-responsive to light, you must contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.
  • Life-Threatening Risks: This symptom can be associated with severe hypertension, internal toxicity, head trauma, or acute glaucoma.
  • Consequences of Delay: Delaying treatment could lead to permanent blindness or further neurological deterioration.

How can a photo of my cat's eyes help the veterinary triage team?

  • Take a clear, high-resolution photo of your cat's eyes in a brightly lit room to provide critical visual data.
  • The photo allows the medical team to see the exact degree of dilation and check for asymmetry between the eyes.
  • Clinicians can look for secondary signs like redness, cloudiness, or hemorrhage before you even arrive.
  • This evidence helps the clinic prioritize your case and prepare specific diagnostic equipment, such as a tonometer or ophthalmoscope.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of acute blindness in cats, dilated pupils with absent or diminished pupillary light reflexes (PLR) can indicate several ophthalmic emergencies. These include sudden acute retinal degeneration syndrome, optic neuritis, and retinal detachment. Intracranial disease affecting the optic chiasm or optic tracts can also manifest as acute blindness with normal PLR. Central blindness, resulting from conditions such as cardiac arrest, seizures, or severe head trauma affecting the optic tracts or occipital cortex, may present with acute blindness and normal PLR. Progressive vision loss with diminished PLR suggests retrobulbar visual tract involvement.

Chapter: Emergency, General Principles, Cardiopulmonary

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1659)

faqs.question: What are common causes for pupils not responding to light?
faqs.answer: Common causes include feline hypertension (high blood pressure), retinal detachment, head trauma, brain tumors, or ingestion of certain toxins like lilies or certain human medications.

faqs.question: Can my cat go blind if their pupils stay dilated?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the underlying cause is high blood pressure or glaucoma, the pressure on the retina or optic nerve can cause permanent blindness within hours if not treated immediately.

faqs.question: My cat is acting normal otherwise, do I still need a vet?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cats are masters at hiding pain and discomfort. Even if they are eating or purring, unresponsive pupils are a significant clinical sign that something is wrong internally.
